does: This paper introduces CITA, a controlled red-teaming framework for evaluating Chinese toxicity in large language models. The method operates through three stages: learning harmful intent from seed examples, enhancing semantic indirectness while preserving core harmful meaning, and applying obfuscation techniques to create surface variants that evade detection. The authors evaluate seven existing toxicity detectors on CITA-generated samples and find they miss harmful content at an average attack success rate of 69.48%, with human evaluation confirming that harmful intent remains intact despite increased implicitness and evasiveness. They demonstrate downstream defensive value by fine-tuning a Chinese Implicit Toxicity Defense model on CITA-generated data, showing that systematic red-team data improves detector robustness.
connects: This work extends adversarial evaluation methodologies developed for English toxicity detection into the under-resourced Chinese setting, addressing a critical gap in multilingual LLM safety research. It builds on prior work in adversarial attacks against content moderation systems and red-teaming frameworks for LLMs, while acknowledging that existing approaches focus primarily on explicit harmful language. The paper responds to findings that current toxicity detection systems rely heavily on surface-level signals and struggle with semantically indirect attacks, problems well-documented in English but underexplored for Chinese. It combines techniques from adversarial NLP with controlled data generation methods previously used in defense-data creation, adapting these approaches to the linguistic and cultural particularities of Chinese toxicity.
enables: This framework enables systematic evaluation of Chinese LLMs against implicit toxicity attacks and provides a foundation for building more robust Chinese content moderation systems. By generating high-quality adversarial data with preserved harmful intent but increased implicitness, CITA supports the development of better-defended models through targeted fine-tuning. The work opens investigation into multilingual toxicity evaluation beyond explicit wording, potentially inspiring similar implicit-attack frameworks for other languages with different linguistic structures and cultural contexts. Future work can leverage CITA-style approaches to identify detector vulnerabilities, prioritize safety research investments in under-resourced languages, and develop more nuanced evaluation benchmarks that account for the combination of semantic indirectness and surface-level obfuscation in toxicity detection.
